Short description: Use politician social-media posts with known party labels to test when human coders can be demoted from primary coders to auditors.
Empirical unit of analysis: Social-media posts paired with public party labels across 11 countries and languages
Method traditions: Computational text analysis; quantitative political science
Observable human actors: Expert coders, crowd workers, RA manager
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; shrinking routine coder
Key typology question: Is routine coding a shrinking task-cluster on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks?
Observable artifacts / traces: Prompt logs, coder disagreement, model outputs, downstream classification stability
Key outcomes observed: Accuracy, macro-F1, downstream inference stability, time saved
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Humans continue to outperform or materially change downstream conclusions on objective tasks
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Task is unusually objective; may not generalize to contested constructs
Why this case has high leverage: Public ground truth makes it one of the cleanest displacement tests available now.
main_limitations: Primary source access is limited; case is usable with explicit caveats and landing-page evidence.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
